usage_note: Slides are differentiated by magnification requirement (20x vs. 40x), layer requirement (single-layer vs. multi-layer Z-stack), specimen size (standard vs. double-width), urgency (priority vs. routine), and tissue type (histology, cytology, IHC).
Slides arrive from upstream lab processes in batches following the tissue processing and staining schedule, rather than as individual independent arrivals.
Strong morning surges from overnight tissue processing; mid-day smaller batches from daytime processing. Many labs separate priority specimens from routine flow.
Priority specimens are processed on a different timing track than routine specimens, with shorter turnaround targets.

QC failures route slides to a safety container for manual re-preparation or rescan — a rework loop back to upstream prep or to a direct rescan. First-pass yield vs. effective throughput metrics distinguish the two.
Scanners run 24 hours in some labs; operators load slides in shift-based availability windows; upstream tissue processors and stainers have their own batch schedules.
In multi-scanner labs, slide-to-scanner routing depends on slide requirements (multi-layer Z-stack requires a capable scanner like the P480) — a state-dependent routing decision.
The scanner and safety container are shared resources across multiple upstream batch streams; contention arises when multiple wards or specialty clinics feed the same central pathology lab.
